summary: French-Algerian author and philosopher. Most commonly identified by The Stranger (L'Étranger), The Plague (La Peste), The Myth of Sisyphus, The Fall, The Rebel, and the short story 'The Guest' from the collection Exile and the Kingdom. Also wrote plays including Caligula, The Just Assassins, and The Misunderstanding. Central to his philosophy: the absurd, and the conclusion that 'one must imagine Sisyphus happy.'

works.name: The Stranger (L'Étranger)
works.description: Novella about the apathetic Meursault who shoots an Arab on a beach and is condemned to death. Opens 'Maman died today.'

clues.clue: Opens 'Maman died today' — about Meursault who shoots an Arab

a man's trial for shooting an Arab

clues.clue: Meursault angrily rejects a chaplain's graces in prison, opening himself to 'the gentle indifference of the world'
examples: an apathetic man angrily rejects a chaplain's graces in favor of opening to 'the gentle indifference of the world'

clues.clue: Preface: protagonist's refusal to lie and 'simplify life' leads society to condemn him; 'annoyance rather than real regret'
examples: protagonist's refusal to lie and 'simplify life' leads a threatened society to condemn him

clues.clue: Salamano treats his dog badly; Raymond has sex with and spits on his girlfriend; Meursault helps write the letter

clues.clue: Contains a newspaper clipping about The Misunderstanding's plot (Martha and mother kill son Jan)

works.name: The Plague (La Peste)
works.description: Novel about Dr. Bernard Rieux fighting a plague outbreak in the quarantined city of Oran, Algeria.

clues.clue: Dr. Bernard Rieux fights plague in Oran; quarantine cuts the city off from the world; Father Paneloux preaches against the disease
examples: Bernard Rieux, a doctor who fights the outbreak of disease in Oran
Father Paneloux preach against a disease that Bernard Rieux struggles to keep contained in Oran

clues.clue: Only two people called 'saints' — old asthmatic Spaniard and old man who tears up paper for cats on balcony
examples: refers to only two people as 'saints;' these are an old, asthmatic Spaniard and an old neighbor who tears up paper on his balcony for cats

works.name: The Myth of Sisyphus
works.description: Philosophical essay arguing that the absurdity of life requires revolt, not suicide. Ends: 'one must imagine Sisyphus happy.'

clues.clue: 'One must imagine Sisyphus happy' — answers 'does the realization of the absurd require suicide?' with no
examples: one must imagine' that the title character is 'happy'
sought to answer the question 'does the realization of the absurd require suicide?'

clues.clue: Uses the conqueror, the actor, the seducer/Don Juan as examples of men who live the ideal absurd life
examples: describes the seducer, the actor, and the conqueror as men who do not try to achieve transcendence
uses the conqueror, the actor, and Don Juan as examples of men who live the ideal life

clues.clue: Argues absence of meaning requires revolt, not suicide
examples: argues that the absence of meaning requires revolt, rather than suicide

works.name: The Fall (La Chute)
works.description: Novel narrated by Jean-Baptiste Clamence, a 'judge-penitent' in Amsterdam's Mexico-City Bar.

clues.clue: Jean-Baptiste Clamence — 'judge-penitent' lawyer at the Mexico-City Bar in Amsterdam; stole The Just Judges panel from Ghent Altarpiece
examples: a lawyer turned 'judge-penitent' who we meet at the Mexico-City Bar in Amsterdam
The Just Judges, a panel of the Ghent Altarpiece, is stolen by the 'judge-penitent' Jean-Baptiste Clamence

works.name: The Rebel (L'Homme révolté)
works.description: Essay arguing that the French Revolution's terror was caused by a misguided quest for 'the end of history.'

clues.clue: Subtitled 'An Essay on Man in Revolt'; argues French Revolution's terror caused by quest for 'end of history'
examples: claimed that the terror of the French Revolution was caused by a misguided quest for 'the end of history'

clues.clue: Attacked Communists in 'Neither Victims nor Executioners'
examples: attacked Communists in the essay series 'Neither Victims nor Executioners'

works.name: Exile and the Kingdom (stories)
works.description: Short story collection containing 'The Guest,' 'The Growing Stone,' 'The Renegade,' and 'Jonas or the Artist at Work.'

clues.clue: 'The Guest' — schoolmaster Daru disobeys gendarme Balducci's orders to deliver an Arab prisoner; finds threat on chalkboard: 'You handed over our brother. You will pay.'
examples: Daru disobeying Balducci's orders
the message 'You handed over our brother. You will pay.' is left on the chalkboard

clues.clue: 'The Growing Stone' — D'Arrast helps a sailor complete a pilgrimage in Brazil; saved from drowning by praying to a stone Jesus

examples: the protagonist kills a missionary to escape being tortured in the House of the Fetish

comprehensive_summary:
Albert Camus was a French-Algerian author and philosopher central to existentialism and absurdism. His most famous works are The Stranger, The Plague, The Myth of Sisyphus, The Fall, and The Rebel.

The Stranger (L'Étranger) opens 'Maman died today' and follows the apathetic Meursault, who helps his neighbor Raymond write a letter, then shoots an Arab on a beach. Salamano mistreats his dog. In prison, Meursault rejects a chaplain's graces and opens himself to 'the gentle indifference of the world.' The novella contains a newspaper clipping about the plot of The Misunderstanding.

The Plague describes Dr. Bernard Rieux fighting a plague in quarantined Oran, Algeria. Father Paneloux preaches against the disease. Only two people are called 'saints': an old asthmatic Spaniard and a man who tears up paper for cats.

The Myth of Sisyphus asks 'does the realization of the absurd require suicide?' and answers no — 'one must imagine Sisyphus happy.' It uses the conqueror, the actor, and the seducer/Don Juan as examples of men living the absurd life.

The Fall features Jean-Baptiste Clamence, a 'judge-penitent' at Amsterdam's Mexico-City Bar who stole The Just Judges panel from the Ghent Altarpiece. The Rebel argues the French Revolution's terror was caused by a misguided quest for 'the end of history.'

Exile and the Kingdom contains 'The Guest' (schoolmaster Daru finds 'You handed over our brother' on his chalkboard after freeing a prisoner), 'The Growing Stone' (D'Arrast helps a sailor in Brazil), 'The Renegade' (torture in Taghaza), and 'Jonas' (canvas reading 'solitary' or 'solidary').

Plays include Caligula (orchestrates his own death; 'men die and are not happy'), The Just Assassins (Kaliayev's assassination of Grand Duke Sergei Alexandrovich), and The Misunderstanding (Martha and mother accidentally kill their son Jan).
